---
title: "15 · Truncated Policy Iteration"
subtitle: "Don't run policy evaluation to convergence; just take K sweeps. Recovers VI as the K=1 case and PI as K=∞."
lesson-id: "mfrl.truncated-policy-iteration"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.policy-iteration"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the VI iteration $v_{k+1} = T^* v_k$ (Lesson 13)?
2. Can you state PI's two steps — exact policy evaluation
   $v_{\pi_k} = (I - \gamma P_{\pi_k})^{-1} r_{\pi_k}$, then greedy
   improvement (Lesson 14)?
3. Are you comfortable with the policy-evaluation operator
   $T^\pi$ as a $\gamma$-contraction (Lesson 12, P1)?

If shaky, revisit Lessons 13 and 14.
:::

## Touch

VI and PI sit at opposite ends of one knob. Here's the knob: how many
*Bellman evaluation steps* $K$ you do per policy improvement.

- $K = 1$: that's value iteration.
- $K = \infty$: that's policy iteration.

Anything in between is *truncated PI*. Slide the knob, watch convergence
on a 3×3 grid.

```{ojs}
viewof Kvalue = Inputs.range([1, 50], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Inner-loop steps K"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof outerIter = Inputs.range([0, 10], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Outer iterations"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const N = 3;
  const goal = [2, 2];
  const gamma = 0.9;
  const next = (i, j, a) => {
    if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) return [i, j];
    let ni = i, nj = j;
    if (a === 0 && i > 0) ni = i - 1;
    if (a === 1 && i < N - 1) ni = i + 1;
    if (a === 2 && j > 0) nj = j - 1;
    if (a === 3 && j < N - 1) nj = j + 1;
    return [ni, nj];
  };
  const reward = (i, j) => (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1] ? 1 : 0);

  // Init policy "always up"
  let policy = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
  let v = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));

  // K Bellman evaluation steps for fixed policy
  function evalK(policy, v, K) {
    for (let it = 0; it < K; it++) {
      const vn = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
        const a = policy[i][j];
        const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
        vn[i][j] = reward(ni, nj) + gamma * v[ni][nj];
      }
      v = vn;
    }
    return v;
  }
  function improve(v) {
    const p = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
      let best = -Infinity, ba = 0;
      for (let a = 0; a < 4; a++) {
        const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
        const q = reward(ni, nj) + gamma * v[ni][nj];
        if (q > best) { best = q; ba = a; }
      }
      p[i][j] = ba;
    }
    return p;
  }

  for (let outer = 0; outer < outerIter; outer++) {
    v = evalK(policy, v, Kvalue);
    policy = improve(v);
  }

  const arrow = a => ["↑", "↓", "←", "→"][a];
  let vmax = 0.001;
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) vmax = Math.max(vmax, Math.abs(v[i][j]));
  const cells = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
    const intensity = Math.round((Math.abs(v[i][j]) / vmax) * 200);
    const colour = `rgb(${255 - intensity}, ${255 - intensity / 2}, 255)`;
    const sym = (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) ? "★" : arrow(policy[i][j]);
    cells.push(html`<div style="background:${colour}; width:80px; height:80px; display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; font-family:monospace; border:1px solid #999;">
      <div style="font-size: 0.85rem;">${v[i][j].toFixed(2)}</div>
      <div style="font-size: 1.4rem;">${sym}</div>
    </div>`);
  }
  return html`<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(${N}, 80px); gap:0; width:max-content; margin:1rem auto;">${cells}</div>
  <div style="text-align:center; font-size:0.9rem; color:#666;">K = ${Kvalue}, after ${outerIter} outer iterations.</div>`;
}
```

Try $K = 1$ (this is VI). Try $K = 50$ (close to PI). Try $K = 5$. The
intermediate $K$ often gets to a usable policy in surprisingly few outer
iterations.

## Wonder

1. With small $K$, each outer iteration is cheap but you make many of
   them. With large $K$, each is expensive but few suffice. Is there a
   sweet spot? Does it depend on the MDP?
2. With $K = 1$ you only have time for a single Bellman step before
   improving. Why does that already work?
3. PI has a clean termination criterion ($\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$). VI's
   stopping rule is $\|v_{k+1} - v_k\|_\infty < \varepsilon$. What's the
   right rule for truncated PI?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — The two extremes

Show that:
(a) Truncated PI with $K = 1$ is *exactly* value iteration.
(b) Truncated PI with $K = \infty$ (or "evaluate to convergence") is
*exactly* policy iteration.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For (a), what does one Bellman evaluation step give? Then what does the
greedy improvement do?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(a)** With $K = 1$ and starting $v_0$: $v_1 = T^{\pi_0} v_0$. Then improve:
$\pi_1 = $ greedy w.r.t.\ $v_1$. Then evaluate one step: $v_2 = T^{\pi_1} v_1$.
Now $T^{\pi_1} v_1$ at state $s$ is
$\sum_a \pi_1(a|s)[r + \gamma \sum P v_1] = \max_a [r + \gamma \sum P v_1] = T^* v_1$
(because $\pi_1$ is greedy). So $v_2 = T^* v_1 = T^* T^{\pi_0} v_0$, and in
general $v_{k+1} = T^* v_k$ — value iteration.

**(b)** With $K = \infty$, the inner loop converges to $v_{\pi_k}$ (the
exact value of the current policy by Lesson 12). That's PI.

**Truncated PI is one algorithm with a knob from VI to PI.**
</details>

### Problem 2 — Why does $K \ll \infty$ still converge?

Argue that for any *fixed* $K \ge 1$, truncated PI converges to $v^*$ as
the outer iterations $\to \infty$, provided $\gamma < 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Show the outer iteration's "effective operator" — call it $\tilde T_K$ —
is a contraction. The contraction constant might be larger than $\gamma$
but should still be $< 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\hat v_k$ be the value after $K$ inner steps starting from $v$ under
the greedy policy $\pi$ of $v$. So $\hat v_k = (T^\pi)^K v$ where $\pi$ is
greedy w.r.t.\ $v$. The outer step is $v \mapsto \hat v_k$. After improvement
the new policy is greedy w.r.t.\ $\hat v_k$. One can show (Puterman 1994,
§6.5) that the resulting "modified PI" iteration produces values that
monotonically increase and converge to $v^*$ at a rate at least
$\gamma^K$ per outer iteration. Geometrically, $K$ trades a single outer
step against $K$ inner steps; total work is comparable. **Convergence is
guaranteed for any $K \ge 1$.**
</details>

### Problem 3 — Per-iteration cost

The cost per outer iteration of truncated PI is $\Theta(K \cdot |S|^2 |A|)$
(approximately). The number of outer iterations to reach $\varepsilon$
accuracy is $\Theta(\log(1/\varepsilon)/(K(1-\gamma)))$ (heuristically).
Find the $K$ that minimises *total work*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total work $\propto K \cdot \log(1/\varepsilon)/(K(1-\gamma)) = \log(1/\varepsilon)/(1-\gamma)$.
The $K$ cancels — what does that mean?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Asymptotically the $K$ cancels: total work is the same for any $K$. **In
practice** the cancellation is not exact: small $K$ wastes work because the
greedy improvement is based on poor evaluations; large $K$ wastes work
because the policy hasn't changed much yet. Empirically $K \in [3, 10]$
is the sweet spot. **The truncated version usually wins**, even though the
worst-case bound is indifferent.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Stopping criterion

Suggest a stopping criterion for truncated PI that uses both the policy
and the value.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
PI stops when $\pi$ stops changing. VI stops when $v$ stops changing.
What if both?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Stop when **either** $\pi$ stops changing **or** $\|v_{k+1} - v_k\|_\infty < \varepsilon$.
- Policy stability is exact: $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$ implies $v_k = v^*$ and we
  could exit.
- Value stability handles the case where $\pi$ keeps oscillating between
  near-optimal policies (e.g.\ ties in argmax).

In practice many implementations also check $\|T^* v - v\|_\infty < \varepsilon$
(the "Bellman residual"), which directly bounds the suboptimality of the
returned policy.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Wall-clock comparison

For a 10-state MDP with $\gamma = 0.9$ and target accuracy $10^{-3}$,
estimate the number of *Bellman backups* required by VI ($K = 1$), PI
($K \approx 50$, single matrix solve $\sim 10^3$ Bellman backups), and
truncated PI ($K = 5$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **VI.** Backups per outer = $|S|^2 |A| \approx 100 \cdot |A|$. Outer
  iterations $\approx \log(10^{-3})/\log(0.9) \approx 65$. Total $\approx 6500\,|A|$.
- **PI.** Each outer iteration solves an $|S| \times |S|$ system,
  $\Theta(|S|^3) = 10^3$ backup-equivalents. Outer iterations: typically
  3-5. Total $\approx 5000\,|A|$.
- **Truncated PI ($K = 5$).** Inner sweeps: 5. Outer iterations:
  $\approx 65 / 5 = 13$ (roughly). Total $\approx 13 \cdot 5 \cdot 100 |A| = 6500\,|A|$.

All three are roughly comparable. With well-tuned $K$ and warm starts on
$v$, truncated PI typically wins by 2-3× on real problems.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
The lesson of truncated PI is the *generalised policy iteration* template:
**any** mix of partial evaluation and partial improvement converges, as
long as both steps move you in the right direction. This template
underwrites every actor-critic algorithm in modern RL.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Truncated Policy Iteration (TPI).** Choose $K \ge 1$, tolerance
$\varepsilon$. Initialise any deterministic $\pi_0$ and any $v_0$. Repeat
for outer iterations $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$:

1. **$K$ Bellman evaluation steps:** for $j = 1, \ldots, K$,
   $$v_j(s) \leftarrow r(s, \pi_k(s)) + \gamma\!\sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s, \pi_k(s))\,v_{j-1}(s')\quad \forall s,$$
   with $v_0 = v_k$ (warm start).
2. **Greedy improvement:**
   $$\pi_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \arg\max_a\!\Big[r(s, a) + \gamma\!\sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s, a)\,v_K(s')\Big].$$
3. Set $v_{k+1} = v_K$.

Stop when $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$ or $\|v_{k+1} - v_k\|_\infty < \varepsilon$.

$K = 1$ recovers VI; $K = \infty$ recovers PI.

:::

A verse:

> *Sweep a few times to estimate;*
> *Then take a greedy improvement step.*
> *VI and PI sit at the ends of one dial —*
> *Tune K, and the rest is the same algorithm.*

## Apply

### Worked example — Truncated PI ($K = 3$) on the 2-state MDP from Lesson 14

Same MDP as Lesson 14's worked example. $\gamma = 0.5$. Initial
$\pi_0 = (a, a)$ (both stay), $v_0 = (0, 0)$.

**Outer iter 1.**
- *Inner eval (K=3) under $\pi_0$.* Both stay, reward 0. $v_1 = v_2 = v_3 = (0, 0)$. (One specialised case: rewards are zero so values stay zero.)
- *Improve.* Same as Lesson 14 iter 1: $\pi_1 = (b, b)$.

**Outer iter 2.**
- *Inner eval (K=3) under $\pi_1 = (b, b)$.* $v_0 = (0, 0)$.
  $v_1(s_1) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1, v_1(s_2) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 2$.
  $v_2(s_1) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 2, v_2(s_2) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 2.5$.
  $v_3(s_1) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2.5 = 2.25, v_3(s_2) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 3$.
- *Improve.* $q(s_1, a) = 0.5 \cdot 2.25 = 1.125$. $q(s_1, b) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 3 = 2.5$. So $\pi_2(s_1) = b$.
  $q(s_2, a) = 0.5 \cdot 3 = 1.5$. $q(s_2, b) = 2 + 0.5 \cdot 2.25 = 3.125$. So $\pi_2(s_2) = b$.
- $\pi_2 = \pi_1 = (b, b)$ ⇒ **stop**.

Final $v \approx (2.25, 3.0)$. The *exact* $v^* = (8/3, 10/3) \approx (2.67, 3.33)$.
We're at 84% of optimal in 2 outer iterations × 3 inner sweeps = 6 backup
ops, vs.\ PI's exact 2 outer iterations × 1 matrix solve. **The values are
slightly off, but the policy is already optimal.** Continuing the inner
loop further refines $v$ without changing $\pi$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Repeat the worked example with $K = 1$ (this is VI). After 2 outer
iterations, what's $v$ and is the policy optimal?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$K = 1$ means one Bellman step per improvement. Like the worked example
but with the inner loop truncated to one sweep.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Outer 1: $v_0 = 0$, $\pi_0 = (a, a)$. Eval (1 step): still $(0, 0)$.
Improve: $\pi_1 = (b, b)$. So $v_1 = (0, 0)$.
Outer 2: Eval under $\pi_1$ (1 step): $v(s_1) = 1, v(s_2) = 2$. Improve:
$q(s_1, b) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 2 > q(s_1, a) = 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$. $\pi_2(s_1) = b$.
Similarly $\pi_2(s_2) = b$. **Policy already optimal after 2 outer
iterations**, but $v = (1, 2)$ is far from $v^* = (8/3, 10/3)$.
</details>

**W2.** What does the inner loop's *warm start* (using $v_k$ rather than
restarting from 0) buy you?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
After the first outer iteration, $v_k$ is already a decent estimate of
$v_{\pi_{k+1}}$ — the policy hasn't changed *much*. Starting the inner
loop from $v_k$ rather than 0 means the inner loop converges in fewer
sweeps. Warm starts are why truncated PI is fast in practice — they
amortise across outer iterations.
</details>

**W3.** Why doesn't truncated PI need the matrix inversion that PI uses?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
PI uses $v_\pi = (I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} r_\pi$ — matrix inversion costs
$O(|S|^3)$ and is intractable for large $|S|$. Truncated PI replaces it
with $K$ Bellman backups, each $O(|S|^2)$, total $O(K|S|^2) \ll O(|S|^3)$
for small $K$. **This is what makes PI's framework usable for medium-scale
MDPs.**
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** *Choose $K$ adaptively.* Suppose at outer iteration $k$ the policy
hasn't changed for two consecutive iterations. Should you increase or
decrease $K$? Why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
If $\pi$ has stabilised, the bottleneck is $v$. More inner sweeps will
tighten $v$ without changing the policy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Increase $K$.** A stable policy means greedy improvement isn't producing
new policies — additional outer iterations are wasted. But more inner
sweeps refine $v$ for the (now-stable) $\pi$, hastening true convergence.
This is the heuristic behind "modified policy iteration" (Puterman 1994):
adaptive $K$ that grows as $\pi$ stabilises.
</details>

**P2.** Run truncated PI ($K = 2$) on the 4-state corridor of Lesson 13 P1.
Compare backup count to plain VI.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the corridor: 4 states, reward 1 at $s_3$ (absorbing), $\gamma = 0.5$.
Start with $\pi_0 = $ "left."
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi_0 = $ "left" is the worst possible: $s_3$ stays (absorbing), but
$s_0, s_1, s_2$ all flee away from the goal (or stuck at $s_0$).

Outer 1: $v$ via 2 inner sweeps. Outcome: $v(s_3) = 1.75$ (built up over 2
absorbing self-loops $0 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0$, then $1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1$,
then $1 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 1.5$, etc. — actually 2 sweeps gives $1.5$). All
else 0. Improve: $\pi_1 = $ "right" everywhere except trivial states.

Continued. After 4-5 outer iterations × 2 inner sweeps = 10 backups, you
reach the optimal policy and approximately $v^*$. Plain VI on this problem
needs $\sim 12$ iterations to reach 3-digit accuracy. So truncated PI
matches or beats VI here. **The win is bigger when $|S|$ grows.**
</details>

**P3.** Show that the convergence guarantee of truncated PI does *not*
require the inner loop to converge — only that $T^\pi$ is a contraction
and you do at least $K \ge 1$ steps. (Sketch.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The outer-step operator $\tilde T_K$ defined by
$\tilde T_K(v) = (T^{\pi_v})^K v$ where $\pi_v = $ greedy w.r.t.\ $v$,
maps fixed points of $T^*$ to themselves and inherits the contraction-like
properties from both $T^\pi$ and $T^*$. Specifically, one shows
$\|\tilde T_K v - v^*\|_\infty \le \gamma \|v - v^*\|_\infty$ — the contraction is
preserved no matter how small $K$ is, *provided* the greedy improvement
step is exact. This is Puterman's "modified policy iteration" theorem
(1994 §6.5.4).
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Asynchronous truncated PI.* Suppose you do $K$ inner sweeps but
only update *one randomly chosen state* per sweep. Does it still converge?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is "Gauss-Seidel" PI. As long as every state is visited infinitely
often, the contraction in $\ell_\infty$ still applies (proved by Bertsekas
& Tsitsiklis 1989).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes, provided every state is updated infinitely often. The proof uses
asynchronous-iteration theorems for monotone contractions — see Bertsekas
& Tsitsiklis (1989), *Parallel and Distributed Computation*, Ch. 6.
**This is the foundation of distributed/parallel RL planners and of
prioritised sweeping.**
</details>

**S2.** Truncated PI with $K = 1$ is VI; with $K = \infty$ is PI. What is
truncated PI with **fractional $K$**, e.g.\ "do half a Bellman sweep"?
Does it converge?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Half a Bellman sweep could mean *partial state coverage*: update only
half the states per outer iteration. That's the asynchronous case (S1)
with one specific schedule.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
"Fractional $K$" makes sense as updating a *subset of states* per outer
iteration. The S1 result covers it: as long as every state is updated
infinitely often, convergence holds. **In practice this is "prioritised
sweeping" (Moore & Atkeson 1993): focus updates on states whose Bellman
residual is largest.** It's an industrial-strength heuristic that's
often 10× faster than vanilla VI.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The "don't fully solve the inner problem before moving on" trick appears
in almost every alternating-optimisation algorithm in machine learning. EM
algorithms truncate the M-step. ADMM and proximal methods do partial
minimisations. Even gradient descent on neural networks can be viewed as
"truncated" coordinate descent. Puterman (1994) was the first to call out
that this is the *same trick* that gives truncated PI.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"VI and PI are different algorithms."** They're the $K = 1$ and
   $K = \infty$ ends of one algorithm. Truncated PI is the natural
   parameterisation.
2. **"You need $K$ very large for convergence."** No — *any* $K \ge 1$
   converges. Larger $K$ converges in fewer outer iterations but each is
   more expensive.
3. **"The contraction constant gets worse with smaller $K$."** Per outer
   iteration, yes ($\gamma^K$ vs.\ $\gamma$). But total work is
   comparable.
4. **"Async updates break the convergence proof."** They don't (S1).
   Async only requires that every state be updated infinitely often.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **Truncated PI = VI ($K = 1$) and PI ($K = \infty$) on one knob.**
2. **$K \in [3, 10]$ is the empirical sweet spot** for medium-state MDPs.
3. **Generalised PI** template (any partial eval + any partial improve)
   underwrites every actor-critic algorithm.
4. **Async / prioritised sweeps** preserve convergence and often speed it
   up by an order of magnitude.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you write the truncated PI pseudocode without notes?
2. Did you verify by hand that $K = 1$ truncated PI is *exactly* VI?
3. Can you name two RL algorithms (later in this track) that follow the
   generalised PI template?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 16. The model-based half of the track is
  done; sample-based RL begins.
- **2 yes:** redo P1 (adaptive $K$) — it captures the deep idea.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problems 1 and 2.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **Lesson 14 (PI).** Provides the evaluate-improve loop.
- **Lesson 13 (VI).** Provides the special case $K = 1$.
- **Lesson 12 (BOE contraction).** Underwrites convergence for any $K$.

### What this unlocks

- **All sample-based RL methods (Lessons 17 onward).** They replace inner
  Bellman steps with MC averages or TD updates. The outer
  evaluate-improve template stays.
- **Actor-critic methods (Lessons 35-37).** "Generalised policy
  iteration" with function approximation.
- This lesson **completes the model-based story** of Part 1. Everything
  after assumes you don't know $P$.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** "Generalised policy iteration" is the conceptual umbrella
under which Sutton & Barto unified the entire field. PPO, A2C, SAC, IMPALA,
DQN — every modern algorithm sits inside this template, with different
choices for the *evaluation* and *improvement* substeps.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Operations research / planning.** Truncated PI (under names like
"modified PI" or "approximate PI") is the workhorse algorithm for
medium-scale MDPs in production: power grid control, supply-chain
planning, robotic motion planning. Its sweet spot $K \in [3, 10]$ is
folklore.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning. The sentence
"VI and PI are the same algorithm with different inner-loop counts"
captures truncated PI's content.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §4.3. Cite freely; never transcribe.
- Puterman 1994 §6.5 for the modified-PI framework and the convergence
  proof for arbitrary $K$.
- Touchstones: Sutton & Barto's "generalised PI" framing; Bertsekas &
  Tsitsiklis 1989 for asynchronous iteration theorems.
:::
